structured_choices.data_source: De-identified EHR data from a large hospital system spanning 36 months of ICU and non-ICU admissions. Raw data includes timestamped vital signs (every 1–4 hours), laboratory results (sporadic, typically 1–3 per day), medication administrations with timestamps, nursing events/interventions, and clinical notes. Data is extracted from a production EHR system with institutional oversight and audit controls.
structured_choices.label_definition: Sepsis onset is defined using validated clinical criteria: presence of suspected infection (positive culture or clinical diagnosis code) AND systemic inflammatory response meeting SIRS or qSOFA thresholds within a 12-hour window, with charted onset time recorded prospectively by clinicians or inferred from the earliest timestamp where all criteria are simultaneously met in the EHR. The prediction target is binary: sepsis onset within the next 6 hours (1) vs. no sepsis within 6 hours (0). Any admission with sepsis onset is censored at the onset time; observations beyond onset are excluded. Admissions without sepsis over the full hospitalization are labeled negative.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split with held-out future months. Months 1–24 are used for training. Months 25–30 are used for validation (hyperparameter tuning and model selection). Months 31–36 are held-out test data, never touched during development. This temporal strategy prevents look-ahead bias and ensures both models are evaluated on data from a future period unseen during training, matching the deployment scenario where the model must predict on new admissions. The split respects the chronological nature of the task and avoids trivial temporal leakage.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (imputation strategies, scaling, encoding mappings) are fit exclusively on the training set (months 1–24). These fitted transformers are then applied identically to validation and test sets. Missingness imputation uses forward-fill and carry-forward-last-observation within admission, then median imputation by ward/admission type fitted only on training data. Vital sign and lab value outliers are clipped at 99th percentiles computed on training data only. Categorical encodings (medication, procedure, diagnosis codes) are one-hot encoded using the vocabulary observed only in training. This prevents information leakage from future data into preprocessing decisions.
structured_choices.features: Sequence model input: For each admission, extract a 24-hour sliding window of observations preceding each 6-hour prediction time. Raw features are: (1) Vital signs: heart rate, respiratory rate, systolic/diastolic BP, temperature, SpO₂ (interpolated to 1-hour regular intervals or represented as sparse irregular timestamps with duration-since-last-measurement indicators); (2) Labs: WBC, lactate, creatinine, bilirubin, platelet count, glucose (represented as latest value + time-since-measurement + count of measurements in window); (3) Medications: IV fluids volume, vasopressors, antibiotics (binary flags and cumulative dose); (4) Nursing events: interventions (intubation, catheter placement), charted sepsis concerns. Baseline static features: Per-admission summary statistics including minimum, maximum, mean of vitals over last 24 hours; latest lab values; cumulative medication counts; age, sex, admission source, primary diagnosis code, comorbidity count (Elixhauser). Both models receive identical input information — only the representation (sequence vs. aggregated) differs.
structured_choices.model: Time-aware sequence model: LSTM or Transformer-based architecture taking as input the 24-hour window of timestamped events (vitals, labs, meds, nursing events) and producing a 6-hour ahead binary sepsis probability. Specific architecture: 2-layer bidirectional LSTM with 128 hidden units, attention pooling, and a final 2-layer dense head (128 → 64 → 1 sigmoid). Hyperparameters tuned on validation set: learning rate, dropout, layer size, sequence padding strategy.
structured_choices.baseline: Static logistic regression model trained on the same 24-hour aggregated features (min/max/mean vitals, latest labs, medication counts, demographics, comorbidities). Logistic regression is a fair baseline because it: (1) uses the same information set as the sequence model (just pre-aggregated); (2) is given equivalent tuning effort via validation set hyperparameter search (regularization strength, class weighting); (3) represents the standard of care / current practice in many settings. Both are trained with identical class weights to handle sepsis incidence imbalance (~2–5% positive rate). The baseline is not a trivial rule; it is a well-tuned statistical model.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Recall at a clinically calibrated false-positive rate (FPR) threshold. Specifically: recall at the operating point where FPR ≤ 0.10 (i.e., no more than 1 false alert per 10 normal admissions). This aligns with stakeholder priorities: the goal is to catch sepsis cases early (high recall) while keeping alert burden acceptable (bounded FPR). The metric is computed on the held-out test set only, after validation-based threshold selection. Justification: Raw accuracy is misleading when sepsis is rare. AUROC alone does not encode the operational constraint on false alerts. Recall@FPR≤0.10 directly reflects the clinical trade-off: catching deterioration early enough to intervene vs. alert fatigue.
secondary_metrics: AUROC: overall discrimination ability; should improve if sequence model genuinely captures temporal patterns.
Precision at the same threshold: to confirm false-alert burden is acceptable.
Recall at additional FPR thresholds (0.05, 0.15): to show sensitivity of findings to threshold choice.
Calibration curve (predicted probability vs. observed frequency): to assess whether confidence scores are trustworthy for clinical use.
Per-stratum metrics: stratified by admission type (ICU vs. non-ICU), time-of-day, and comorbidity burden to identify if improvements are real or driven by subgroup shifts.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Validation set (months 25–30) is used for all hyperparameter tuning and model selection. A held-out validation split within months 1–24 (e.g., 20% of training admissions, stratified by sepsis status and admission type) is used for early stopping during training. The test set (months 31–36) is never accessed during development; a single evaluation run is performed at the end.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is held completely untouched during all development and validation phases. After both models are finalized on the validation set, they are evaluated once on the held-out test data (months 31–36). A single evaluation report is generated; the test set is not re-used for threshold tuning or post-hoc model adjustments. This ensures an unbiased estimate of generalization performance.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Known confounds are addressed as follows: (1) **Missingness patterns**: Both models receive the same raw data with identical missingness; preprocessing (imputation) is fit on training only and applied uniformly. Any difference in handling sparse labs or vitals is controlled by giving both models the same feature representation and imputation strategy. (2) **Care-process changes over time**: The temporal split (months 1–24 training, 31–36 test) means that any systematic improvement in sepsis recognition or treatment protocols over time affects both models equally; the test data reflects real future conditions. If sequence model outperforms baseline in this test period, it is not simply capturing historical shifts. (3) **Sepsis incidence imbalance**: Both models are trained with class weights proportional to inverse incidence. Threshold selection for the baseline and sequence model is done independently but both are evaluated at the same FPR threshold, ensuring a fair comparison. (4) **Label noise and onset-time uncertainty**: Admissions where sepsis onset time is ambiguous (e.g., no charted time, or conflicting clinical signs over several hours) are flagged and excluded from analysis; a sensitivity analysis retrains on relaxed inclusion criteria to assess robustness. (5) **Subgroup effects**: Stratified analysis by ICU admission, time-of-day, age group, and comorbidity burden checks whether improvements are broad-based or confined to specific populations. If the sequence model improves dramatically only for a small subgroup, this raises questions about generalization or confounding by subgroup-specific changes in practice. (6) **Leakage from future events**: Any event or charted code recorded more than 6 hours after prediction time is excluded from feature windows. Vitals and labs are timestamped; any recorded after the 6-hour horizon is removed.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Time-Aware Sepsis Prediction in EHR Data

### 1. Objective and Data Strategy

The hypothesis posits that a time-aware sequence model will outperform a static baseline when predicting 6-hour sepsis onset. To test this rigorously, we would use 36 months of de-identified EHR data from a large hospital system, spanning ICU and non-ICU admissions. The raw data include timestamped vital signs (recorded every 1–4 hours), laboratory results, medication administrations, nursing events, and clinical documentation. This real-world EHR data reflects the operational conditions where the model will be deployed.

**Label Definition:** Sepsis onset is defined using clinically validated criteria: suspected infection (positive culture or clinical diagnosis) co-occurring with SIRS or qSOFA thresholds within a 12-hour window. The onset time is the earliest timestamp at which all criteria are simultaneously met. The prediction target is binary: sepsis onset within 6 hours (positive) vs. no sepsis within 6 hours (negative). Any admission with sepsis is censored at onset time; observations beyond are excluded to respect the temporal ordering. Admissions without sepsis over the full stay are labeled negative. Approximately 2–5% of admissions are expected to be positive, reflecting clinical incidence.

### 2. Split Strategy and Leakage Prevention

We employ a strict **temporal split** to prevent look-ahead bias and match the real deployment scenario:
- **Months 1–24:** Training set. Used to fit the sequence model, the baseline, and all preprocessing transformers.
- **Months 25–30:** Validation set. Used for hyperparameter tuning and model selection (threshold selection for both models).
- **Months 31–36:** Held-out test set. Evaluated once at the end; never accessed during development.

This temporal split is essential because the task is inherently sequential: a model deployed in month 31 must be evaluated on data it has never seen before. A random split would allow future information (and any systematic care-process improvements) to leak into training, inflating performance estimates. The 12-month gap between training and test ensures both models face a genuinely held-out future period.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Both models receive the same underlying information; they differ only in representation:

**Raw Features:**
- Vitals: heart rate, respiratory rate, blood pressure, temperature, SpO₂ (interpolated or represented as irregular timestamps with time-since-last-measurement).
- Labs: WBC, lactate, creatinine, bilirubin, platelet count, glucose (latest value + time-since-measurement + count in window).
- Medications: IV fluid volume, vasopressor cumulative dose, antibiotic flags.
- Nursing events: intubation, catheterization, charted interventions.
- Demographics: age, sex, admission source, primary diagnosis, comorbidity count.

**Sequence Model Input:** A 24-hour sliding window of timestamped events preceding each 6-hour prediction time.

**Baseline Input:** Summary statistics over the same 24-hour window (minimum, maximum, mean of vitals; latest lab values; cumulative medication counts; demographics).

**Preprocessing (fit on training only, applied to val/test):**
- Missingness: forward-fill within admission, then median imputation by ward and admission type (fitted on training data only).
- Outliers: vital sign and lab values clipped at 99th percentiles computed on training data.
- Categorical encoding: one-hot encoding using vocabulary observed only in training.
- Scaling: standardization fitted on training set, applied to validation and test.

This strategy prevents information leakage from future data into preprocessing and ensures the baseline and sequence model face identical feature quality.

### 4. Model, Baseline, and Tuning

**Sequence Model:** A 2-layer bidirectional LSTM (128 hidden units per layer) with attention-based pooling, feeding a 2-layer dense head (128 → 64 → 1 sigmoid). This architecture is expressive enough to capture temporal dependencies in vital signs, lab trends, and event sequences.

**Baseline Model:** Logistic regression trained on the aggregated 24-hour features. Logistic regression is a fair baseline because it:
- Uses identical information to the sequence model (pre-aggregated).
- Receives equivalent tuning effort via validation-set hyperparameter search (regularization strength C, class weighting).
- Represents current clinical practice in many settings.

Both models are trained with class weights inversely proportional to sepsis incidence to handle the ~2–5% positive rate.

**Hyperparameter Tuning:** On the validation set (months 25–30), we perform grid search over learning rate, dropout, layer size (sequence model) and regularization strength (baseline). Model selection criterion: Recall at False Positive Rate ≤ 0.10 (see metrics below).

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary Metric:** Recall at a clinically calibrated False Positive Rate (FPR) threshold. Specifically, **recall at the operating point where FPR ≤ 0.10**. This aligns directly with stakeholder priorities: catching sepsis cases early (high recall) while keeping alert burden manageable (at most 1 false alert per 10 normal admissions). The threshold is selected on the validation set and fixed for test evaluation.

**Justification:** Raw accuracy is misleading when sepsis is rare. AUROC alone does not encode the operational constraint on false alerts. This metric directly reflects the clinical trade-off: sensitivity vs. alert fatigue.

**Secondary Metrics:**
- AUROC: overall discrimination (should improve if temporal modeling is effective).
- Precision at the same threshold: confirms false-alert burden is acceptable.
- Recall at additional FPR levels (0.05, 0.15): sensitivity to threshold choice.
- Calibration curve: predicted probabilities vs. observed frequency (important for clinical trust).
- Per-stratum metrics: recall and FPR stratified by ICU status, admission time, comorbidity burden (ensures improvements are not driven by subgroup shifts).

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Usage

During development, a 20% hold-out validation split within months 1–24 (stratified by sepsis status and admission type) is used for early stopping. The full validation set (months 25–30) is used for hyperparameter tuning and threshold selection. The test set (months 31–36) is **never accessed** until final evaluation. After both models are finalized, they are evaluated once on the test set. A single evaluation report is generated; no re-tuning or cherry-picking occurs post-hoc.

### 7. Controls for Confounds

**Missingness & Feature Handling:** Both models receive the same raw data and undergo identical preprocessing (fit on training only). Any difference in performance is not due to differential imputation strategies.

**Care-Process Changes:** The temporal split ensures that both models benefit equally from any secular improvements in sepsis recognition or treatment over months 31–36. If the sequence model outperforms the baseline in this future period, it is not a statistical artifact of historical changes.

**Imbalance & Threshold Bias:** Both models are trained with class weights and evaluated at the same FPR threshold, ensuring a fair comparison at an operationally meaningful operating point.

**Label Noise:** Admissions with ambiguous or conflicting sepsis onset times are flagged and excluded from the primary analysis. A sensitivity analysis retrains on relaxed inclusion criteria to assess robustness to labeling uncertainty.

**Subgroup Confounding:** Stratified evaluation by ICU admission, time-of-day, age, and comorbidity checks whether improvements generalize or are confined to specific populations. A broad improvement across strata strengthens the claim that temporal modeling is genuinely helpful; dramatic improvement in a single subgroup raises concerns about subgroup-specific confounds.

### 8. Interpretation and Scope

This design will establish whether the sequence model achieves higher recall at a clinically acceptable false-alert rate than the baseline when both are evaluated on future, unseen admissions. It does **not** establish whether the improvement is clinically meaningful in a real deployment (that requires a prospective trial). It does establish that temporal information, when properly represented, can improve prediction over aggregated statistics in a retrospective cohort.
